content:
# Deuteronomy 2 — Disagreement Log
**Chapter:** Deuteronomy 2 (37 verses)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA
**Total disagreements logged:** 15

---

## V.1 — "Reed Sea" vs. "Red Sea"

- **EMET:** "by the way of the Reed Sea"
- **LOGOS:** "by the way of the Red Sea"
- **PNEUMA:** "on the road to the Red Sea"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "Red Sea" — project convention for Yam Suph in the running text.

---

## V.1 — "went around" vs. "circled around" vs. "wandered...for a long time"

- **EMET:** "we went around Mount Se'ir many days"
- **LOGOS:** "we circled around Mount Seir many days"
- **PNEUMA:** "we wandered for a long time around the hill country of Seir"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "went around Mount Seir many days" — plain, faithful to *va-nasov*. PNEUMA's "wandered" and "hill country" are interpretive additions.

---

## V.4 — "sons of Esau" vs. "children of Esau" vs. "descendants of Esau"

- **EMET:** "sons of Esav"
- **LOGOS:** "children of Esau"
- **PNEUMA:** "descendants of Esau"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "sons of Esau" — project convention for *bney* genealogical formula.

---

## V.5 — "Do not provoke" vs. "Do not contend with them" / "foot's tread" vs. "footstep" vs. "single foot"

- **EMET:** "Do not provoke them...a foot's tread"
- **LOGOS:** "Do not contend with them...a footstep"
- **PNEUMA:** "Do not provoke them to war...so much as a single foot"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "Do not provoke them...a foot's tread" (EMET). "Provoke" captures *titgar* adequately; "foot's tread" preserves the idiom most faithfully.

---

## V.7 — "He has known your walking" vs. "He knows your wandering" vs. "He has watched over your journey"

- **EMET:** "He has known your walking through this great wilderness"
- **LOGOS:** "He knows your wandering through this great wilderness"
- **PNEUMA:** "He has watched over your journey through this vast wilderness"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "He has known your walking through this great wilderness" (EMET). The Hebrew *yada' lekhtekha* uses the intimate covenant-knowing (*yada'*) with the specific noun *lekheth* (walking). "Watched over" (PNEUMA) smooths away the theological weight of *yada'*.

---

## V.9 — "harass" vs. "trouble" / "provoke war" vs. "contend in battle"

- **EMET:** "Do not harass Mo'av and do not provoke war against them"
- **LOGOS:** "Do not harass Moab, and do not contend with them in battle"
- **PNEUMA:** "Do not trouble Moab or provoke them to war"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "Do not harass Moab and do not provoke war against them" (EMET). Two distinct Hebrew verbs (*al-tatsar* / *al-titgar*) should produce two distinct English phrases. "Harass" and "provoke war" are the most precise pair.

---

## V.15 — "to destroy them" vs. "to confound them" vs. "to root them out"

- **EMET:** "to destroy them from the midst of the camp"
- **LOGOS:** "to confound them from the midst of the camp"
- **PNEUMA:** "to root them out from the camp until they were gone"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "to root them out from the midst of the camp until they had perished." "Root them out" (PNEUMA) best renders *le-hummam* (thoroughgoing destruction); "perished" from EMET preserves the closing *ad tummam*.

---

## V.18 — present tense formulation

- **EMET:** "You are passing over today the border of Mo'av, [past] Ar"
- **LOGOS:** "Today you are to cross the border of Moab at Ar"
- **PNEUMA:** "Today you are to cross the border of Moab, past Ar"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "Today you are crossing the border of Moab, past Ar." Present continuous reflects the immediacy; "past Ar" (PNEUMA/EMET) is preferred over "at Ar" (LOGOS).

---

## V.24 — battle engagement verb

- **EMET:** "and provoke war against him"
- **LOGOS:** "and contend with him in battle"
- **PNEUMA:** "wage war against him"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "engage him in battle" — a clear, direct rendering that neither under- nor over-translates *hit'gar bo*.

---

## V.25 — "tremble and writhe" — addition of "in anguish"

- **EMET:** "shall tremble and writhe because of you"
- **LOGOS:** "shall tremble and writhe because of you"
- **PNEUMA:** "will tremble and writhe in anguish before you"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "shall tremble and writhe because of you" (EMET/LOGOS). PNEUMA's "in anguish" is an addition not in the MT.

---

## V.30 — hardening construction

- **EMET:** "hardened his spirit and made his heart stubborn"
- **LOGOS:** "hardened his spirit and made his heart obstinate"
- **PNEUMA:** "had made him stubborn and hardened his heart"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "hardened his spirit and made his heart obstinate" (LOGOS). The MT preserves two verbs with two distinct nouns (*ruach* / *lev*); PNEUMA's version collapses this distinction. "Obstinate" (LOGOS) is marginally better than "stubborn" (EMET) for *imets levavo*.

---

## V.34 — "devoted to destruction" vs. "completely destroyed" / "little ones" vs. "children"

- **EMET:** "devoted to destruction every city — men and women and little ones"
- **LOGOS:** "devoted them to destruction — men, women, and children"
- **PNEUMA:** "completely destroyed every city — men, women, and children"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "devoted them to destruction — every city: men, women, and children." "Devoted to destruction" (EMET/LOGOS) preserves the *cherem* theological term. "Children" adopted over "little ones" for clarity.

---

## V.37 — "sons of Ammon" vs. "children of Ammon" vs. "Ammonites" / restriction formulation

- **EMET:** "the land of the sons of Ammon...all that the LORD our God commanded us"
- **LOGOS:** "the land of the children of Ammon...whatever the LORD our God had forbidden to us"
- **PNEUMA:** "the land of the Ammonites...anywhere else the LORD our God had forbidden"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "sons of Ammon" per project convention. "All that the LORD our God had forbidden to us" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) — "forbidden" is more contextually accurate than EMET's "commanded" for this boundary restriction.

---

## V.26 — "words of peace" vs. "a peaceful offer"

- **EMET:** "with words of peace"
- **LOGOS:** "with words of peace"
- **PNEUMA:** "with a peaceful offer"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "with words of peace" (EMET/LOGOS). The Hebrew *divrei shalom* is a formulaic diplomatic expression; it should not be paraphrased.

---

## V.29 — "until I cross the Jordan/Yarden"

- **EMET:** "until I cross the Yarden"
- **LOGOS:** "until I cross the Jordan"
- **PNEUMA:** "until I cross the Jordan"
- **SOPHIA ruling:** "until I cross the Jordan" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) — conventional English form per project convention.
